summary:
The Sa Huynh Culture (c. 500–200 BC) was a prehistoric culture of central and southern Vietnam, distinct from the northern [[event:1176|Dong Son Culture]], characterized by distinctive jar burials and a trade network extending across maritime Southeast Asia that may represent early Austronesian (Cham) ancestors.

Sa Huynh people (named for a site in modern Quang Ngai province) practiced a distinctive burial rite of placing cremated remains in large pottery jars, often accompanied by glass beads of a particular bicephalic (two-headed) animal type found across Southeast Asian maritime trade networks. The presence of these distinctive beads in the Philippines, Borneo, and other islands indicates that Sa Huynh merchants or migrants were active participants in maritime trade networks across Southeast Asia. The Sa Huynh culture is now generally accepted as ancestral to the Cham people who dominated central Vietnam from the 2nd to 15th centuries AD, making the Sa Huynh period crucial for understanding the deep roots of Austronesian presence on the mainland. Their iron-working skills and maritime orientation distinguished them from the northern Dong Son tradition.
